texts.orig: Gas-liquid membrane contactor is a mass transfer device for gas absorption in which the microporous hydrophobic hollow fiber membranes are generally employed as the phase barrier. The membrane provides the contact between gas and liquid phases making these two phases flow independently. Gas-liquid membrane contacting process offers the advantages over the conventional contactors, i.e., packed, plate and sprayed columns, that it can overcome flooding, foaming and channeling problems and provides very high contact area per unit volume [1,2]. In addition, scaling up is more straight forward. However, the membrane adds the additional resistance to the overall mass transfer, especially in case of the partially wetted mode where the membrane pores are partially penetrated by the liquid absorbent.
texts.text: Gas-liquid membrane contactor is a mass transfer device for gas absorption in which the microporous hydrophobic hollow fiber membranes are generally employed as the phase barrier. The membrane provides the contact between gas and liquid phases making these two phases flow independently. Gas-liquid membrane contacting process offers the advantages over the conventional contactors, i.e., packed, plate and sprayed columns, that it can overcome flooding, foaming and channeling problems and provides very high contact area per unit volume [1,2]. In addition, scaling up is more straight forward. However, the membrane adds the additional resistance to the overall mass transfer, especially in case of the partially wetted mode where the membrane pores are partially penetrated by the liquid absorbent.

texts.orig: The analytical solutions for calculating the average wetting ratio, x n , for gas-liquid membrane contacting process based on log-normal and normal distribution functions have been developed. By using the membrane characteristics, liquid absorbent properties and operating conditions, the average wetting ratio is determined analytically. The overall mass transfer coefficient ( KG ) corresponding to the average wetting ratio is also calculated. Due to the pressure drop of liquid flow in the fiber, the operation mode would be changed along the fiber and six possible situations of operation cases are observed. In addition, the proposed calculation can predict the fiber lengths that are completely wetted, partially wetted and are completely dry. In order to verify the proposed calculation, the experiments of CO2 absorption from the gas mixture (CO2-N2) by polyvinylidenefluoride (PVDF) hollow fiber membrane contactor using water and monoethanolamine (MEA) as the absorbents were performed at various inlet liquid pressures ( PL0 ) and liquid velocities. The results obtained from the experiments were reported in term of overall mass transfer coefficient and were validated with those obtained from the proposed calculations. It reveals that calculation based on log-normal distribution shows a better fit with the experiments compared to that based on normal distribution at all operating conditions indicating that the membrane pore structure can be described better using log-normal distribution function. In addition, the calculated average wetting ratio based on log-normal distribution function greed well with that obtained from the numerical model proposed in the literature.
texts.text: The analytical solutions for calculating the average wetting ratio, x n , for gas-liquid membrane contacting process based on log-normal and normal distribution functions have been developed. By using the membrane characteristics, liquid absorbent properties and operating conditions, the average wetting ratio is determined analytically. The overall mass transfer coefficient ( KG ) corresponding to the average wetting ratio is also calculated. Due to the pressure drop of liquid flow in the fiber, the operation mode would be changed along the fiber and six possible situations of operation cases are observed. In addition, the proposed calculation can predict the fiber lengths that are completely wetted, partially wetted and are completely dry. In order to verify the proposed calculation, the experiments of CO2 absorption from the gas mixture (CO2-N2) by polyvinylidenefluoride (PVDF) hollow fiber membrane contactor using water and monoethanolamine (MEA) as the absorbents were performed at various inlet liquid pressures ( PL0 ) and liquid velocities. The results obtained from the experiments were reported in term of overall mass transfer coefficient and were validated with those obtained from the proposed calculations. It reveals that calculation based on log-normal distribution shows a better fit with the experiments compared to that based on normal distribution at all operating conditions indicating that the membrane pore structure can be described better using log-normal distribution function. In addition, the calculated average wetting ratio based on log-normal distribution function greed well with that obtained from the numerical model proposed in the literature.

The effect of these parameters on membrane wetting is usually described by Laplace-Young Eqs. (2), (4), (6), (8) and (9) which provides the trans-membrane pressure difference that the liquid can penetrate the membrane pores. The average pore radius of the membrane is usually used to calculate the penetration pressure [8,9]. Due to the pore size distribution, the pores with size larger than the average pore radius are possible to be wetted. Therefore, the membrane pore size distribution should be considered in order to predict the pore wetting more accurately.

texts.orig: The mathematical models investigating the degree of membrane wetting and the effect of membrane wetting on the absorption efficiency have been widely proposed [17-20]. Wang et al. [21] developed the two dimension model to predict CO2 absorption and validated the model with the experimental results. Mavroudi et al. [22] proposed the first-order expression to describe the membrane resistance change with time for physical absorption of pure CO2. Khaisri et al. [23] developed the mathematical model for investigating mass and heat transport based on the resistance-in-series model to describe the influence of wetting on the process performance. Faiz and Al-Marzouqi [17] presented the model considering pseudo wetting on the absorption performance for CO2 absorption at high pressure. Although the modeling study on the absorption of CO2 by gas-liquid membrane contacting process have been investigated widely for the past decade, however, several models presented the approximation of the membrane wetting as the fitting parameter. The calculations of membrane wetting did not include all important parameters affecting membrane wetting. To the best of our knowledge, there is no the published work on the analytical calculation of the membrane wetting. Basically, the analytical solution is more convenient compared to the iteration methods because the simulation tool is not needed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aim of this work is to present the analytical solutions for predicting average wetting ratio, x n by taking the membrane characteristics, liquid absorbent properties as well as operating conditions into consideration. The mass transfer equations together with the Laplace-Young equation considering the membrane pore size distribution based on log-normal and normal distribution functions were solved analytically. The proposed calculation is also able to predict the change of operation mode along the fiber length and determine the lengths of individual operation mode. In order to verify the proposed calculation, the experiments of CO2 absorption by PVDF hollow fiber membrane contactor using water and MEA solution as the absorbents were performed at different liquid inlet pressures ( PL0 ), liquid velocities and MEA concentrations and were validated with the proposed calculation. Additionally, the calculated average wetting ratio based on log-normal and normal distribution functions were compared to that obtained from the numerical model proposed in our previous work [9].

The proposed calculation is also able to predict the change of operation mode along the fiber length and determine the lengths of individual operation mode. Additionally, the calculated average wetting ratio based on log-normal and normal distribution functions were compared to that obtained from the numerical model proposed in our previous work [9].

texts.orig: The experimental set up of the gas-liquid membrane contactor is illustrated in Fig. 2. In this work, all experiments were performed at room temperature (around 25 1 C). The gas flow rate supplied from the compressed gas cylinder was controlled by the mass flow controllers, (Brooks Model 4800 series). The set point controller (Brooks Model 0254) was used to control and monitor the flow rate of gas entering the module. In the experiments, the gas mixture was fed through the shell side of the membrane module, counter-currently to the absorbent flow into the tube side of the fibers. A gear pump (Cole-Parmer model 75211-15) delivered the absorbent from the reservoir to the membrane module. The inlet and outlet concentrations of CO2 in gas phase were analyzed by the Gas analyzer (Servomex, 1440). The data were collected after the experiments reached the steady state (around 15 min). The results of each run were averaged from three samplings. The absorption fluxes were calculated from the measurements of CO2 concentration of gas phase and the gas flow rate at the inlet and outlet of membrane module. The overall mass transfer coefficient ( KG ) can be determined from the experimental results based on the concentration in the gas phase by the following equation [28]:

From the result, the normal distribution of the PVDF membrane used in this work presents only the positive value of pore radius which is reasonable to describe pore size distribution. By considering the definitions of both distribution functions, the membrane pore volume calculated by the integration of Eq. (15) would be performed from 0 to N and from � N to N for the log-normal and normal distribution functions, respectively. However, in order to represent the real pore size distribution that the pore radius starts at rmin and the biggest pore radius presents at rmax , the integration should be performed from rmin to rmax . Although the curves presented by these two distribution functions show a slight distinctive shape due to a small value of geometric standard deviation ( s is 0.13 for log-normal distribution and is 0.125 for normal distribution), the properties of these two distribution functions are different. The particular expressions of pore characteristic for each distribution function would present the differences in degree of pore wetting and wetting behavior at various trans-membrane pressure differences.

texts.orig: Fig. 4 illustrates the increase of wetting ratios, x n L and x n N with increasing trans-membrane pressure difference ( D PL � G ). The wetting normally starts at the biggest pores and then magnifies to the smaller pores once the trans-membrane pressure difference increases. From the pore size distribution data (Fig. 3), the values of the minimum and maximum pore radii are around 0.05 and 0.18 m m, respectively. With water (viscosity of 8.96 -10 � 4 Pa s) flow in the lumen side of the fiber, by using Eq. (24), the wetting begins at D PL � G approximately 0.52 bar and the complete wetting occurs at D PL � G around 1.88 bar. It is clearly seen that the calculated x n L is greater than x n N at any D PL � G because x n N increases more slowly with D PL � G at the beginning of wetting compared to x n L . Considering the shape of wetting ratio, it can be seen that at the values of D PL � G close to 0.52 (the start of wetting) and 1.88 bar (near complete wetting), the wetting ratio increases slightly with D PL � G due to the small number of pores with radii around rmax and rmin . At the middle of wetting, an increase of D PL � G significantly increases wetting ratio due to a high density of pore distribution around rm . For instance, when D PL � G is increased from 0.9 to 1.0 bar, the calculated x n L increases from 0.61 to 0.87 and while x n N increases from 0.29 to 0.61. These obviously reveal that the wetting ratio and wetting behavior relate to the pore size distribution characteristic.

texts.orig: In this work, the average wetting ratio for entire length of fiber is determined analytically. In order to obtain the correct value of average wetting ratio, the average trans-membrane pressure difference D PL � G , ln �  which is the cause of membrane wetting must be determined properly. As mentioned previously, D PL � G , ln determined by Eq. (26) can be used for the system with single mode operation, i.e., only completely wetted or partially wetted or completely dry mode, for the entire fiber length. However, due to the pressure drop, the operation mode may change along the fiber. For example, near the liquid inlet, the operation is in the partially wetted mode. Due to the high pressure drop of liquid flow, the operation may be changed to the dry mode towards the end of fibers. For such case, the use of Eq. (26) to calculate average wetting ratio is not correct. Therefore, in order to obtain the correct wetting ratio, the real operation modes along the fiber must be figured out. Fig. 5 illustrates the possible operation modes (without bubble formation) along the fiber length. The bubble formation would occur for operation under the line of D PL � G ¼ D PBub . The completely dry mode could be performed between the lines D PL � G ¼ D PBub and D PL � G ¼� 2 g cos y = r max. The operation carried out in the range of D PL � G ¼� 2 g cos y = r max to D PL � G ¼� 2 g cos y = r min would be in the partially wetted mode. It can be seen that the system can be performed in the dry mode (Case (f)) at PL0 lower than 1.52 bar. However, the dry mode can be carried out until the liquid velocity is around 2.35 m/s because of the lower limit operating line which is the operation with the outlet pressure 1 bar. At PL0 between 1.52 and 2.88 bar and low liquid velocity, the system is operated in partially wetted mode (Case (d)). The system will change to the region combining partially wetted and dry modes (Case (e)) at higher liquid velocity because of the high pressure drop resulting in low D PL � G at the outlet. Thus, the pores are gasfilled towards the end of fiber. The membrane pores are completely wetted (Case (a)) at PL0 higher than 2.88 bar and will change to Case (b) at higher liquid velocity. The operation of Case (c) is observed at very high liquid velocity ( v L 4 6.04 m/s) and PL0 higher than 2.88 bar. The operation mode will change from completely wetted mode to dry mode along the fiber and the pressure drop over the fiber must be very high. Additionally, for the system with fixed PL0 , the wetting ratio decreases with increasing liquid velocity which is observed from the decrease of the length of liquid-filled pore (lengths of partial wetting or complete wetting zones) with increasing liquid velocity. For instance, at PL0 2 bar, the value of Lpw / L changes from 0.8 to 0.6 when the liquid velocity increases from 2.64 to 3.52 m/s. Also, in Case (b), the fiber consists of complete wetting and partial wetting parts, an increase of liquid velocity leads to the increase in Lpw and the decrease in Lcw resulting in lower wetting ratio.

texts.orig: Fig. 8 depicts the regions of different operation cases for the system with fixed liquid outlet pressure ( PLf ). For this operation, the wetting ratio will increase with increasing liquid velocity. The reason is that with fixed the pressure at liquid outlet, high liquid velocity results in high pressure drop and high pressure at liquid inlet. These cause higher LPW (Case (e)), LCW (Case (b)) and wetting ratio. In practice, the operation of gas-liquid membrane contacting process is performed at the liquid pressure as low as possible (without bubble formation). This operation is located on the line of lower limit operating line presented in Figs. 7 and 8. However, the system can be operated in the dry mode in the limit range of operation (0 o v L o 2.35) because of the pressure drop. The range of dry mode will be narrower for the system with longer fiber due to higher pressure drop for the same liquid velocity.

texts.orig: conditions, the over estimations are observed indicating that calculated wetting ratios are lower than those existed in the experiments. This is found by the points over the 45 1 line presented in Fig. 9(ii). The deviations between the experimental and calculated data may be due to the assumption that the shape of the membrane pores is cylindrical. Indeed, it is known that the membranes fabricated via phase inversion have tortuous pores which as observed from the cross sectional image [33]. However, the deviation is quite small for the calculation based on log-normal distribution. This is because for the pore size distribution measurement using a capillary flow porometer, the Laplace-Young equation is applied to determine the equivalent pore radius at various trans-membrane pressure differences. Thus, even though the membrane pores are tortuous, the real pore size distribution obtained from this technique can be equivalent to the distribution of the cylindrical pores. In case of the calculation based on normal distribution function, a larger deviation is observed since this function does not represent the real structure of the membrane pores. This reveals that the selection of the distribution function to describe membrane pores structure can add considerable error in predicting membrane wetting and absorption performance. Thus, it can be concluded that the wetting ratio as well as overall mass transfer coefficient can be predicted well using the membrane pore structure obtained from the log-normal distribution.

texts.orig: Fig. 10 compares the experimental and calculated KG and x n based on log-normal and normal distribution functions at different operation cases for physical absorption. The dot symbol represents experimental KG , the solid line represents calculated KG , while x n is illustrated by the dashed line. For Exp. 1, KG and x n calculated from these two different distribution functions are the same line because the experiments were performed in dry mode ( x n L and x n N ¼ 0). An increase of liquid velocity significantly improves KG because the overall resistance is dominated by liquid phase. In case of Exp. 2, the experiments were carried out in the condition located on the lower limit operating line which occupies the regions of case (e) ( v L o 2.32 m/s) and case (f) ( v L 4 2.32 m/s). It can be seen that KG remarkably enhances with liquid velocity for the liquid velocity up to approximately 2.32 m/s and then, KG decreases with increasing liquid velocity because the membrane pores become partially wetted. The similar results were also investigated by Malek et al. [34] that the Sherwood number increased with the Graetz number and reached the maximum value, and then decreased with increasing liquid flow rate. The maximum value is dependent on both Graetz number and wetting pressure. The system with higher wetting pressure (smaller pore radius) presents the maximum Sherwood number at higher Graetz number. Even though x n L and x n N observed from Exp. 2 are smaller than 0.001 at the liquid velocity higher than 2.32 m/s, the decrease of KG is significant. For these conditions, the improving of KG with liquid velocity is less than the effect of membrane wetting. Basically, the gas diffusion in the gas-filled membrane pores depends on the combination of molecular and Knudsen diffusions, while that of liquid-filled membrane pores depends on diffusivity of gas in liquid phase. In the liquid-filled membrane pores, the diffusion coefficient of CO2 is much lower than that in the gas-filled pores. The values of diffusivities of CO2 in gas-filled ( DCO 2 � G ) and liquidfilled ( DCO 2 � H2O ) pores presented in Table 2 reveals that the ratio of diffusivity in the gas-filled pores to that of the completely liquidfilled pores is approximately 8000:1 at room temperature. For Exp. 3 and Exp. 4, the results calculated from different distribution functions are obviously different. The average wetting ratio calculated from normal distribution function is lower resulting in higher KG,N compared to KG,L . The wetting ratio also decreases with increasing liquid velocity because the experiments were carried out with fixed PL0 . As discussed previously, the calculation based on normal distribution shows high deviation compared to the experiments at these conditions, while the log-normal distribution function still show a good fit with the experiments. For Exp. 5, the wetting ratios calculated from both log-normal and normal distribution functions are higher than 0.999 at all liquid velocities studied. The system was performed at the condition very close to completely wetted mode, thus, KG observed from these experiments are also low. In Exp. 6, the experiments were run at completely wetted mode, thus, KG and x n calculated from these two different distribution functions are on the same line. From the results carried out at various operation cases, it can be seen that the calculation based on normal distribution function can predict the results well for the conditions close to dry and completely wetted modes but it presents over estimation for the condition of partially wetting mode. Whereas, the calculation based on log-normal distribution function shows a good prediction for all operation cases. Therefore, it can be concluded that the log-normal distribution function is suitable for using to describe the pore characteristic of the PVDF hollow fiber membrane used in this work. An uncertainty in the selection of distribution function in describing the membrane pore characteristic results in uncertainty in predicting the membrane wetting as well as process performance.

texts.orig: Fig. 11 compares the average wetting ratio calculated based on log-normal and normal distribution functions in this work with those given by a numerical model from our previous work [9]. The average wetting ratio obtained from the numerical model was determined by adjusting the value of x n in the model until the absorption flux obtained from the simulation was very close to or equal to the experimental data in which the difference did not exceed 0.1%. The use of the average wetting ratio as a fitting parameter has been widely applied in the literature [17,22,23]. The results show that the average wetting ratios obtained from the numerical simulation are very close to and present the same trend to those calculated based on log-normal distribution.

texts.orig: The analytical solutions based on log-normal and normal distribution functions have been developed to predict the membrane wetting, x n , as well as overall mass transfer coefficient ( KG ) for absorption of CO2 by PVDF hollow fiber membrane contactor. The wetting ratio is determined theoretically as function of membrane characteristics, liquid absorbent properties as well as operating conditions. By considering the liquid inlet pressure ( PL0 ) or liquid outlet pressure ( PLf ) and liquid velocity, the length of individual operation mode including the lengths of completely wetted, partially wetted and dry parts of the fiber can be estimated. Due to the pressure drop of liquid flow in the lumen side, six possible operation cases can be observed. In order to verify the proposed calculation, the experiments were carried at the different operation cases. The results obtained from the experiments were validated with those from the calculations. It is shown that the calculation based on normal distribution function can predict the results well for the conditions close to dry and completely wetted modes but it presents over estimation for the condition of partially wetting mode. While, the calculation based on log-normal distribution function shows a good prediction for all operation cases. Furthermore, the calculated average wetting ratio based on log-normal distribution function greed well with that obtained from the numerical model proposed in our previous work [9]. This reveals that log-normal distribution function is suitable for describing the pore size distribution of the membrane and also gives good prediction of the average wetting ratio.
texts.text: The analytical solutions based on log-normal and normal distribution functions have been developed to predict the membrane wetting, x n , as well as overall mass transfer coefficient ( KG ) for absorption of CO2 by PVDF hollow fiber membrane contactor. The wetting ratio is determined theoretically as function of membrane characteristics, liquid absorbent properties as well as operating conditions. By considering the liquid inlet pressure ( PL0 ) or liquid outlet pressure ( PLf ) and liquid velocity, the length of individual operation mode including the lengths of completely wetted, partially wetted and dry parts of the fiber can be estimated. Due to the pressure drop of liquid flow in the lumen side, six possible operation cases can be observed. In order to verify the proposed calculation, the experiments were carried at the different operation cases. The results obtained from the experiments were validated with those from the calculations. It is shown that the calculation based on normal distribution function can predict the results well for the conditions close to dry and completely wetted modes but it presents over estimation for the condition of partially wetting mode. While, the calculation based on log-normal distribution function shows a good prediction for all operation cases. Furthermore, the calculated average wetting ratio based on log-normal distribution function greed well with that obtained from the numerical model proposed in our previous work [9]. This reveals that log-normal distribution function is suitable for describing the pore size distribution of the membrane and also gives good prediction of the average wetting ratio.
